2025 · EMNLP · Post-training & RL

DeepResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Reinforcement Learning in Real-world Environments

The first open framework to train a research agent end-to-end with reinforcement learning against the live web — not a sanitized RAG corpus — and the planning, cross-validation, reflection, and honest abstention that emerge from outcome-only reward.

Zheng, Fu, Hu, Cai, Ye, Lu, Liu
venue EMNLP 2025 citations 220 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-21
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By early 2025 "deep research" agents — Gemini Deep Research, OpenAI Deep Research, Grok's DeeperSearch, and open projects like MetaGPT, OpenManus, and OWL — could already chain web searches into multi-step investigations. But the open ones were prompt-engineered: hand-built workflows that hard-code how the model should search, read, and decide. That makes them brittle, weak at generalizing to novel tasks, and dependent on endless manual prompt tuning; the commercial systems that work better are completely opaque.

The obvious fix is to train the behavior rather than script it, and reinforcement learning had just shown big reasoning gains (DeepSeek-R1, Kimi k1.5). The open attempts to marry RL with search — Search-R1, R1-Searcher, ReSearch — did so inside a controlled local RAG sandbox: the agent "searches" a fixed Wikipedia corpus through a retriever. That setting bakes in a crippling assumption — that every answer already lives in the corpus. Real research doesn't work like that. Information is missing, outdated, or scattered across domains the corpus never covered; pages are noisy, heterogeneous, and actively hostile (anti-crawling, rate limits, latency).

So the gap is concrete: nobody had scaled RL for a research agent against the actual open web. DeepResearcher is the first to do it end-to-end — and to argue that real-environment training isn't an implementation detail but a prerequisite for robustness.

// how it works

The method

The backbone is Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, trained with GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) in the verl framework. GRPO drops the critic: for each question it samples a group of rollouts and uses their mean reward as the baseline, so the advantage of a trajectory is just how much better it did than its siblings. Each training step samples 256 prompts × 16 rollouts, and each rollout may make up to 10 tool calls before answering.

A trajectory is an interleaved loop: the model <think>s, emits a JSON web-search call (top-k=10, returning title/URL/snippet for each hit), reads the <tool_response>, optionally fires a browse call to read full pages, and finally commits an <answer>. Crucially, browsing is delegated to a dedicated multi-agent reading module: a browsing agent keeps a per-query short-term memory, reads a page segment by segment like a human skimming, decides at each step whether to keep reading or stop, and appends only the relevant extract. That is the opposite of RAG, which dumps pre-chunked passages — and it lets the agent bail early on unproductive pages.

Reward: outcome-only, with a format gate

There is no process reward and no learned reward model. The signal is simply the word-level F1 of the final answer against the gold short answer — gated by format: malformed output (missing tags, broken structure) is hard-penalized at −1. And because tool responses are observations the model shouldn't learn to generate, every tool-output token is masked from the loss.

reward =   + F1(answer, gold)  if format is valid
 − 1  if format is invalid // outcome-only reward over masked rollouts — the model is graded on the answer, never on the retrieved text

Making live-web RL physically possible

The unglamorous engineering is what makes the idea work. A GRPO batch fires thousands of concurrent searches and crawls (e.g. ~4096 at once), so they stand up a 50-node distributed CPU cluster just to service tool requests during rollout. Anti-crawling and provider rate limits (e.g. 200 req/s) are absorbed with robust retries plus a 7-day search cache — which also slashes Google Search API cost. The segment-wise reading agent handles the long, messy pages real search returns.

Curating search-dependent data (the quiet rigor)

No open training set for deep research existed, so they repurpose open-domain QA: NaturalQuestions and TriviaQA (single-hop) plus HotpotQA and 2WikiMultiHopQA (multi-hop). The catch is contamination — if a question is already in the model's parametric memory, it'll "succeed" without ever searching. Two filters fix this: (1) drop time-sensitive, subjective, or harmful questions (judged by DeepSeek-R1), and (2) a pass@10 contamination screen — sample 10 base-model answers per question and discard any the model can already answer correctly without search. The survivors — 80,000 examples at a 1:1:3:3 ratio (75% multi-hop) — are genuinely search-dependent.

// what they found

The results

Across seven open-domain QA benchmarks, DeepResearcher posts gains of up to +28.9 points over prompt-engineering baselines and up to +7.2 points over RAG-based RL agents. Two metrics are reported: word-level F1 (the training reward) and MBE, a GPT-4o-mini "correct/incorrect" judge meant to forgive correct-but-reworded answers.

In-domain (NQ, TQ, HotpotQA, 2Wiki), it tops MBE on all four: NQ 61.9, TQ 85.0, HotpotQA 64.3, 2Wiki 66.6 — pulling clearly ahead on TQ (vs R1-Searcher's 79.1) and 2Wiki. The honest wrinkle: on NQ and HotpotQA the RAG-trained Search-r1-base is right alongside it (60.0 / 63.0) — but it had a retriever pointed straight at the relevant Wikipedia corpus, while DeepResearcher had to find everything on the open internet.

Out-of-domain (MuSiQue, Bamboogle, PopQA) is where the story lands: DeepResearcher leads both F1 and MBE on all three. The cleanest signal is Bamboogle, whose answers aren't all on Wikipedia — there DeepResearcher hits 72.8 MBE versus R1-Searcher's 65.6 (the headline +7.2) and Search-r1-base's 57.6. Tellingly, even when R1-Searcher (trained on local RAG) is given real-web access at inference, it still trails badly — so it's training in the real environment, not merely deploying there, that builds the skill.

MethodEnvTQ2WikiBamboogle†PopQA†
DeepResearcherlive web85.066.672.852.7
R1-Searcherlive web79.165.865.643.4
Search-r1-baselocal RAG76.247.957.647.0
Search-o1live web69.537.753.643.4
CoT (no search)—48.227.321.615.0

// MBE accuracy. † = out-of-domain (not in training). Best per column in bold.

Training dynamics — it learns to search more, and think longer

Performance scales smoothly with RL: F1 climbs from 0.375 to roughly 0.55 over training. As it learns, the agent makes more tool calls (the 4-hop curve is still rising past step 34) and writes longer reasoning (response length grows from ~2,000 toward 5,000+ tokens) — without saturating. Harder questions pull more search and more deliberation out of the model.

Emergent behaviors from outcome-only RL

With no SFT on planning or verification traces, four research-like behaviors emerge from the reward alone: planning (laying out steps, then merging them when it can), cross-validation (finding the answer on the first call but verifying it anyway before committing), reflection (noticing the results are off-track — e.g. surfacing Denethor when it wanted Djedefhor — and re-querying), and honesty (declining to invent a precise figure, answering "significant portion" rather than hallucinate).

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The systems contribution is real and the engineering is honest. But the evaluation tells a narrower story than the name "deep research" implies, and the central claim leans on cross-setting comparisons rather than a clean ablation.

"Deep research" here is short-answer multi-hop QA. Despite being positioned against Gemini/OpenAI Deep Research — products that write long, cited reports — every training signal and every benchmark is short-answer open-domain QA (NQ, TQ, HotpotQA, 2Wiki, MuSiQue, Bamboogle, PopQA). The reward is literally word-level F1 against a short gold string. So this trains an excellent multi-hop web-QA agent, not a report writer; the authors themselves concede long-form answers would need a "more sophisticated reward." DeepResearch Bench (Du et al., which cites this work) exists precisely to evaluate the long-form task this leaves untouched.

The headline rides on an unvalidated judge. Where F1 and MBE disagree, they lead with MBE — e.g. NQ F1 39.6 (below Search-r1-base's 45.4) but MBE 61.9 (above 60.0); HotpotQA F1 52.8 < 55.9 while MBE leads. Preferring MBE is defensible (word-overlap unfairly dings a web agent's reworded-but-correct answers), but the arbiter — GPT-4o-mini as judge — is never validated against human labels in this paper. A cheap judge can be lenient or self-biased, and the entire ranking hinges on it.

The marquee baseline isn't apples-to-apples. Search-r1-base, the strongest competitor, was trained and evaluated inside a local Wikipedia RAG sandbox with direct corpus access; DeepResearcher fights the open web. The authors flag this and frame it as winning despite a harder setting — fair — but it means no controlled ablation isolates the "real-web RL" effect from confounds (environment, effective data, the multi-agent browser, the curation). There is no same-model, same-data, RAG-vs-live-web head-to-head, so the thesis that real-environment training is a fundamental requirement rests on deltas across different setups, not on a clean knockout.

baselines

Current and relevant (Search-R1, R1-Searcher, Search-o1, CoT/RAG), but the top baseline runs in a Wikipedia sandbox while DeepResearcher fights the open web — honestly disclosed, never ablated apart. Single 7B backbone; no scaling curve; no seed variance on the main tables.

contamination

Above average. A pass@10 base-model screen drops questions the model already knows without search — the right instinct — but only relative to Qwen2.5-7B, and the public dev sets remain heavily web-leaked. Bamboogle (non-Wikipedia, n=125) is the cleanest signal, and it's small.

what's missed

It's short-answer QA graded by word-F1 + a GPT-4o-mini judge — not the long-form, cited reports the "deep research" framing evokes. The showcased "honesty"/abstention is one trajectory, not a measured rate — and an F1/−1 reward actively punishes a non-answer.

would raise confidence

A controlled same-model/same-data RAG-vs-live-web ablation; human validation of the MBE judge; long-form deep-research tasks with grounded grading; larger backbones; multi-seed variance bars.

// bottom line

A genuinely important open milestone: the first reproducible recipe for scaling RL against the real web, with striking emergent planning and reflection and a strong, honest generalization result on Bamboogle. Just read it for what it measures — multi-hop web QA judged by a cheap LLM, not long-form report writing — and note that the flagship "you must train in the real environment" claim is shown by cross-setting deltas rather than a clean ablation.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. First open end-to-end RL recipe for a live-web research agent. Qwen2.5-7B + GRPO, an outcome-only F1 reward (with a −1 format gate) over masked rollouts, trained against real search engines rather than a static RAG corpus.
  2. The decisive evidence is generalization. It leads F1 and MBE on all three out-of-domain sets; on Bamboogle (answers not all on Wikipedia) it beats even a RAG-trained agent handed real-web access — so the training environment, not just the inference environment, is what builds the skill.
  3. Research behaviors emerge from reward alone. Planning, cross-validation, reflection, and honest abstention appear with no SFT on those behaviors; tool-call count and response length keep rising through training without saturating.
  4. The plumbing is the enabler, and the framing is a caveat. A 50-node crawl cluster, retries, a 7-day cache, and a segment-wise reading agent are what make live-web RL feasible — and "deep research" here should be read as short-answer multi-hop QA, scored by F1 plus a GPT-4o-mini judge.
